title: Pithecellobium cyclocarpum
taxonomy: Pithecellobium cyclocarpum is classified under the family Fabaceae. This classification places it within the order Fabales. Furthermore, it belongs to the genus Pithecellobium and the subfamily Mimosoideae. The species is native to the tropical regions of the Americas. Its classification reflects its evolutionary relationships with other plants in the bean and pea family.
uses: Pithecellobium cyclocarpum, commonly known as the Panama tree or Palo de rosa, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The bark and leaves of this tree have been employed to treat fever, cough, and respiratory issues. In some cultures, Pithecellobium cyclocarpum is also used to treat skin conditions and wounds due to its antibacterial properties. Additionally, the tree's bark is believed to have anti-inflammatory properties, which may aid in reducing pain and swelling. More research is needed to fully understand the medicinal uses and potential benefits of Pithecellobium cyclocarpum.

side_effects: Pithecellobium cyclocarpum, also known as the Guanacaste tree, is native to Central America and the Caribbean.  Due to its potential uses in traditional medicine, the tree's side effects are not well-documented. However, it is known to contain a toxic compound called rotenone, which can be hazardous if ingested. intro: Pithecellobium cyclocarpum, also known as the Guanacaste tree, is a species of flowering tree native to the tropical regions of Central America. The Guanacaste tree is valued for its medicinal properties, which include antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial activities that contribute to its potential health benefits, such as reducing the risk of cardiovascular disease, cancer, and infections. The bioactive constituents of Pithecellobium cyclocarpum include flavonoids, terpenoids, and alkaloids, which have been identified as the primary compounds responsible for its medicinal properties.
